title: Multiprocessor Communication, Cache Coherence, Peripheral Devices, Buses and Interrupts
key_concepts: invalid / dirty / shared cache line state

definitions.description: The guarantee that all processors observe a consistent view of shared memory: reads return the last written value, and all writes are seen in the same order by all processors.
When a processor writes to a cache line, all other copies of that line in other caches are invalidated.
When a processor writes to a cache line, all other caches with a copy receive the new value immediately.
A specialized controller that transfers data between a peripheral device and memory directly, bypassing the CPU.
A mechanism by which the CPU suspends its current program to handle an asynchronous event; the interrupted program is resumed afterward.
A table at physical address 0 holding the start addresses of all interrupt service routines; indexed by the interrupt type byte.
An interrupt generated internally by the CPU to signal an abnormal condition (e.g., division by zero).
A circuit output with three states: 0, 1, or high-impedance (electrically disconnected from the bus).

algorithms.name: Interrupt handling sequence
steps: CPU checks IF flag (maskable interrupts only)
CPU responds with INTA; current program suspended; registers saved to stack
Controller places type byte on data bus
CPU reads interrupt vector table at 4×type_byte to find service routine address
Registers restored from stack; IF flag restored; program resumes

examples.name: Write invalidate trace
examples.description: Processor A writes x=5 → A's cache updated, B's copy invalidated; B's next read causes a cache miss and fetches 5.

examples.name: Write update trace
examples.description: Processor A writes x=5 → both A's and B's caches updated to 5 immediately.

examples.name: Interrupt vector table size
examples.description: 256 sources × 4 bytes/address = 1 KB at physical address 0.

learning_objectives: Compare bus and interconnection network communication for multiprocessors
Define and explain the three conditions for memory coherence
Describe snooping and directory-based coherence protocols
Compare write invalidate and write update protocols
Describe how peripheral devices communicate with the CPU
Compare programmed I/O, DMA, and interrupt-driven I/O
Explain bus arbitration and tri-state circuits
Trace the complete interrupt handling sequence
Distinguish hardware interrupts, traps, and software interrupts
